event: Architectural design of an ornate fireplace with symbolic decorative elements
symbols: large ornate wings — evocation of cherubim, the winged guardians of God's presence over the Ark of the Covenant
sphinxes flanking the mantel — guardians of sacred thresholds, echoing the cherubim at Eden's gate and the Temple entrance
fire grate and hearth — sacred fire, divine presence, the burning bush and altar flame
harp-shaped pilasters — instruments of worship, recalling David's psaltery and heavenly praise
symmetrical composition — divine order and cosmic harmony reflecting God's created order
foliate ornament — Tree of Life motif, abundance and eternal life
description: A black-and-white engraving depicting an elaborate neoclassical fireplace design. The central hearth features an ornate metal fire grate with scrollwork and a decorative canopy. Above the mantel, a large pair of spread wings dominates the composition, flanked by decorative urns and foliate scrolls. Two sphinx-like figures recline at the upper corners. The side pilasters are shaped like elongated harps or lyres, adorned with floral garlands. The overall composition is rigidly symmetrical, executed in Piranesi's characteristic precise etching style with fine crosshatching throughout.
theological_description: Though Piranesi's fireplace design is a secular architectural etching, its visual vocabulary draws deeply from the iconography of the Jerusalem Temple. The monumental spread wings crowning the composition unmistakably evoke the cherubim whose wings overshadowed the Mercy Seat atop the Ark of the Covenant (Exodus 25:20), and the enormous cherubim Solomon placed in the Holy of Holies whose wings spanned the entire chamber (1 Kings 6:27). The sphinxes functioning as threshold guardians parallel the cherubim stationed at the east of Eden to guard the way to the Tree of Life (Genesis 3:24). The hearth itself, the locus of fire, carries profound biblical resonance: God appeared to Moses in the burning bush (Exodus 3:2), led Israel as a pillar of fire (Exodus 13:21), and descended upon Sinai in fire (Exodus 19:18). The perpetual fire on the altar was never to go out (Leviticus 6:13). In this reading, the fireplace becomes a domestic altar, a place where the elemental sacred fire is contained within a framework of guardian beings. The harp-shaped pilasters recall the instruments of Levitical worship (1 Chronicles 25:1) and the heavenly liturgy where elders hold harps before the throne (Revelation 5:8). Piranesi, steeped in Roman antiquity, unconsciously channels the ancient Near Eastern and biblical tradition of sacred space: a place where fire burns under the watch of winged protectors, mediating between the human dwelling and the divine presence.

scripture_refs.reason: The dominant spread wings over the mantel visually parallel the cherubim whose wings overshadowed the Mercy Seat on the Ark of the Covenant
Solomon's great cherubim in the Holy of Holies whose wings spanned wall to wall, echoed in the monumental wing motif
Cherubim placed as guardians at Eden's gate parallel the sphinx-like guardian figures flanking the fireplace
The sacred hearth fire evokes the burning bush where God's presence was manifest in flame
The perpetual fire on the altar that was never to go out, paralleled by the domestic hearth as a place of continual flame
The harp-shaped pilasters recall the harps held by the twenty-four elders in heavenly worship
Levitical musicians appointed with lyres and harps for Temple worship, reflected in the lyre-shaped columns
